questions.question:
An organization requires a range of IP addresses to assign one to each of its 1500 computers. The organization has approached an Internet Service Provider (ISP) for this task. The ISP uses CIDR and serves the requests from the available IP address space 202.61.0.0/17. The ISP wants to assign an address space to the organization which will accommodate at least 1500 computers. Which of the following address spaces are potential candidates from which the ISP can allot any one of the organization?

i. 202.61.84.0/21
ii. 202.61.104.0/21
iii. 202.61.64.0/21
iv. 202.61.144.0/21

A. i and ii only
B. ii and iii only
C. iii and iv only
D. i and iv only
questions.to_find: Which /21 blocks can the ISP allot to the organization (must be within 202.61.0.0/17 and properly aligned)

understand.plain: The ISP has a pool of IP addresses: 202.61.0.0/17, which covers 202.61.0.0 to 202.61.127.255. The organization needs at least 1500 IPs, so a /21 block (2048 addresses) is sufficient. The ISP can only give out blocks that lie entirely within its own /17 pool AND are aligned on correct /21 boundaries.

keywords.explain: A method for allocating IP addresses using variable-length subnet masks, written as IP/prefix-length.
keywords.example: 202.61.0.0/17 means the first 17 bits are fixed; 202.61.128.0 is outside this block.

keywords.explain: A /21 block has 2^(32-21) = 2048 addresses. The third octet must be a multiple of 8 for the block to be properly aligned.
keywords.example: 202.61.104.0/21: third octet 104 = 13*8, aligned. 202.61.84.0/21: 84 is not a multiple of 8 (84/8 = 10.5), so NOT aligned.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='560' height='200'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='10' y='10' width='540' height='40' class='hi' rx='6'/><text x='280' y='36' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>ISP Pool: 202.61.0.0/17 (third octet 0-127)</text><rect x='10' y='70' width='120' height='40' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='70' y='86' text-anchor='middle' class='tag no'>i. .84.0/21</text><text x='70' y='102' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>84 not mult of 8</text><rect x='150' y='70' width='120' height='40' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='210' y='86' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>ii. .104.0/21</text><text x='210' y='102' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>104=13*8 OK</text><rect x='290' y='70' width='120' height='40' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='350' y='86' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>iii. .64.0/21</text><text x='350' y='102' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>64=8*8 OK</text><rect x='430' y='70' width='120' height='40' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='490' y='86' text-anchor='middle' class='tag no'>iv. .144.0/21</text><text x='490' y='102' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>144 > 127, out</text><text x='280' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>Valid candidates: ii and iii (aligned + within /17 range)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: ISP pool 202.61.0.0/17 covers third octet 0-127. Candidate i (.84) is not /21-aligned. Candidate iv (.144) is outside the pool. Only ii (.104) and iii (.64) are valid.

given.aim: Find /21 blocks that can be sub-allocated from 202.61.0.0/17 to cover at least 1500 addresses

terms.meaning: ISP's address pool; covers 202.61.0.0 to 202.61.127.255 (17 bits fixed, third octet 0-127)
terms.connects: Any allotted block must start and end within this range.

terms.meaning: Block size = 2^11 = 2048 addresses; meets the 1500-address requirement
A /21 block's third octet must be a multiple of 8 (the block size in the third-octet dimension is 256/32 = 8)
terms.example: 64, 72, 80, 88, 96, 104, 112 ... are multiples of 8; 84 and 144 are not.
terms.connects: Eliminates candidates i (84 not multiple of 8) and iv (144 outside range).

given.plan: For each candidate: (1) check if its third octet is a multiple of 8 (alignment), (2) check if the block's entire range falls within 202.61.0.0 to 202.61.127.255 (containment). Only blocks passing both tests are valid.

steps.title: Check ISP pool range and required block size
steps.formula_raw: Pool: 202.61.0.0/17 => third octet 0 to 127; /21 block => 2^11 = 2048 addresses
steps.apply: The /17 mask fixes the first 17 bits. The third octet ranges from 0 to 127 (bit 17 = 0). A /21 block needs the third octet to be a multiple of 8 for alignment. 2048 >= 1500, so /21 is sufficient.
steps.note: The ISP cannot give out addresses beyond its own pool (third octet <= 127).

steps.title: Test each candidate
steps.formula_raw: Aligned if (third octet mod 8 == 0) AND (third octet + 7 <= 127)
steps.note: Both alignment AND containment in the /17 pool must hold.

solution.result: Only ii (202.61.104.0/21) and iii (202.61.64.0/21) are valid candidates. Answer: B. ii and iii only.

questions.formula_note: CIDR sub-allocation: a block of prefix /p drawn from a parent /q requires the block to be /p-aligned (starting address divisible by 2^(32-p)) and wholly contained within the parent's address range.

questions.question:
Consider routing table of an organization's router shown below:

Subnet Number    | Subnet Mask     | Next Hop
12.20.164.0      | 255.255.252.0   | R1
12.20.170.0      | 255.255.254.0   | R2
12.20.168.0      | 255.255.254.0   | Interface 0
12.20.170.0      | 255.255.254.0   | Interface 1
default          |                 | R3

Which of the following prefixes in CIDR notation can be collectively used to correctly aggregate all of the subnets in the routing table?

A. 12.20.164.0/20
B. 12.20.164.0/22
C. 12.20.168.0/22
D. 12.20.168.0/21
questions.to_find: CIDR prefixes that together aggregate all subnets listed in the routing table

understand.plain: Route aggregation (supernetting) combines multiple contiguous subnets into a single larger block. We need to find a set of CIDR prefixes from the given options that together exactly cover all the explicit subnets in the table (ignoring the default route). Each chosen prefix must cover some subnets and collectively they must cover all subnets without leaving gaps.

keywords.explain: Combining multiple consecutive subnets into one larger CIDR block to reduce routing table size.
Expressing an IP address block as address/prefix-length where prefix-length indicates how many leading bits are fixed.
keywords.example: /22 means 10 host bits free, covering 4 consecutive /24 networks.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='560' height='220'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='280' y='24' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Subnet address space (third octet range)</text><rect x='20' y='40' width='80' height='50' class='hi' rx='4'/><text x='60' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>164-167</text><text x='60' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>/22 (R1)</text><rect x='110' y='40' width='80' height='50' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='150' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>168-169</text><text x='150' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>/23 (If0)</text><rect x='200' y='40' width='80' height='50' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='240' y='62' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ink'>170-171</text><text x='240' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>/23 (R2)</text><rect x='20' y='110' width='80' height='35' class='hi' rx='4'/><text x='60' y='132' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>B: .164.0/22</text><text x='60' y='148' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>covers 164-167</text><rect x='110' y='110' width='170' height='35' class='hi' rx='4'/><text x='195' y='132' text-anchor='middle' class='tag ok'>C: .168.0/22</text><text x='195' y='148' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>covers 168-171</text><text x='280' y='195' text-anchor='middle' class='ink sub'>B + C together cover all three explicit subnets (164-167 and 168-171)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Three subnets: 12.20.164.0/22 (rows 164-167), 12.20.168.0/23 (rows 168-169), 12.20.170.0/23 (rows 170-171). Option B covers the first group; Option C covers the second and third as a /22.

given.aim: Find a minimal set of CIDR prefixes from the options that aggregates all explicit subnets in the table

terms.meaning: Covers third-octet range 164 to 167; already a /22 block
terms.connects: This is the first subnet to be covered by an aggregate.

terms.meaning: Two adjacent /23 blocks covering third-octet 168-169 and 170-171
terms.example: 168+170 are both even, consecutive, so they form a /22.
terms.connects: These two /23 blocks can be aggregated into 12.20.168.0/22.

terms.meaning: Block of 1024 addresses; in third-octet terms covers 4 consecutive octets
terms.connects: Option C (12.20.168.0/22) exactly aggregates the two /23 subnets.

given.plan: Convert subnet masks to prefix lengths. Find the smallest set from {A,B,C,D} such that every explicit subnet (164/22, 168/23, 170/23) is covered by exactly one aggregate, with no overlap or missed subnets.

steps.title: Identify subnets and their ranges
steps.note: The default route is excluded from aggregation; it represents all other traffic.

steps.title: Evaluate each option
steps.formula_raw: Aggregate block covers all member subnets iff each member's range is a subset of the aggregate's range
steps.apply:
A. 12.20.164.0/20: covers .160.0 to .175.255. Overshoots — includes addresses not in any subnet. Not a clean aggregate.
B. 12.20.164.0/22: covers .164.0 to .167.255. Exactly matches the first subnet. Valid.
C. 12.20.168.0/22: covers .168.0 to .171.255. Covers both 168/23 and 170/23. Valid.
D. 12.20.168.0/21: covers .168.0 to .175.255. Overshoots — includes .172-.175 which have no subnets. Not a clean aggregate.
steps.note: An aggregate is valid only if every address in it belongs to one of the subnets being aggregated, ensuring no unintended routes are included.

steps.title: Verify B + C cover all subnets
steps.apply:
B covers 12.20.164.0/22 exactly.
C covers 12.20.168.0/23 and 12.20.170.0/23 exactly.
Together B and C cover all three explicit subnets with no overlap and no extra addresses.
steps.note: This is the correct and minimal aggregation.

solution.result: Options B (12.20.164.0/22) and C (12.20.168.0/22) together correctly aggregate all subnets. Answer: B, C.

questions.formula_note: Route aggregation: two adjacent /n blocks can be merged into one /(n-1) block if they share the same higher-order bits; the merged block's start must be aligned to 2^(32-n+1).

questions.question:
The forwarding table of a router is shown below:

Subnet Number    | Subnet Mask       | Interface ID
200.150.0.0      | 255.255.0.0       | 1
200.150.68.0     | 255.255.252.0     | 2
200.150.68.0     | 255.255.255.0     | 3
200.150.255.0    | 255.255.255.0     | 4
Default          |                   | 0

A packet addressed to a destination address 200.150.68.118 arrives at the router. It will be forwarded to the interface with ID __________.

A. 3
B. 1
C. 2
D. 4
questions.to_find: Interface ID to which a packet destined for 200.150.68.118 is forwarded using longest prefix match

understand.plain: When a router receives a packet, it checks the destination IP against every entry in its forwarding table and picks the most specific match — the entry whose subnet mask has the most bits set (longest prefix match). The packet goes out on the interface of that matching entry.

keywords.explain: The router selects the forwarding table entry whose network prefix is the most specific (longest) match for the destination address.
keywords.example: If both /16 and /24 match a destination, the router picks /24 because it is more specific.

keywords.explain: A 32-bit mask where the network portion is all 1s. ANDing the destination IP with the mask gives the network address.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg' width='560' height='260'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='280' y='24' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>Destination: 200.150.68.118</text><rect x='10' y='40' width='540' height='30' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='20' y='60' class='tag ink'>Subnet</text><text x='180' y='60' class='tag ink'>Mask</text><text x='360' y='60' class='tag ink'>Prefix</text><text x='460' y='60' class='tag ink'>Match?</text><rect x='10' y='75' width='540' height='30' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='20' y='95' class='sub ink'>200.150.0.0</text><text x='180' y='95' class='sub ink'>255.255.0.0</text><text x='360' y='95' class='sub ink'>/16</text><text x='460' y='95' class='tag ok'>YES</text><rect x='10' y='110' width='540' height='30' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='20' y='130' class='sub ink'>200.150.68.0</text><text x='180' y='130' class='sub ink'>255.255.252.0</text><text x='360' y='130' class='sub ink'>/22</text><text x='460' y='130' class='tag ok'>YES</text><rect x='10' y='145' width='540' height='30' class='hi' rx='4'/><text x='20' y='165' class='sub ink'>200.150.68.0</text><text x='180' y='165' class='sub ink'>255.255.255.0</text><text x='360' y='165' class='sub ink'>/24</text><text x='460' y='165' class='tag ok'>YES (longest)</text><rect x='10' y='180' width='540' height='30' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='20' y='200' class='sub ink'>200.150.255.0</text><text x='180' y='200' class='sub ink'>255.255.255.0</text><text x='360' y='200' class='sub ink'>/24</text><text x='460' y='200' class='tag no'>NO</text><text x='280' y='240' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ok'>Longest match: /24 => Interface 3</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Forwarding table lookup for 200.150.68.118: /16 matches, /22 matches, /24 (Interface 3) matches and is the longest prefix, so the packet goes to Interface 3.

given.aim: Apply longest prefix match to destination 200.150.68.118 and determine the output interface

terms.meaning: The IP address of the packet's intended recipient
terms.connects: Each forwarding entry is tested against this address using its subnet mask.

terms.meaning: Matches any address in the range 200.150.68.0 to 200.150.68.255
terms.connects: This is the most specific (longest) match among all matching entries.
A valid match, but less specific than /24; loses to LPM.

given.plan: AND the destination IP with each subnet mask; if the result equals the table entry's subnet number, that entry matches. Among all matching entries, pick the one with the most 1-bits in the mask (longest prefix). Forward to that interface.

steps.title: Test each forwarding table entry
steps.formula_raw: Match if: (destination IP) AND (subnet mask) == subnet number
steps.note: 68 AND 252 = 68 because the lower 2 bits of 68 (binary 01000100) are 00, which are masked out by 252 (11111100).

steps.title: Apply longest prefix match
steps.formula_raw: Choose entry with maximum prefix length among all matching entries
steps.apply:
Matching entries and their prefix lengths:
- Interface 1: /16
- Interface 2: /22
- Interface 3: /24  <-- LONGEST
- Default:      /0

Longest prefix = /24 => Interface 3.
steps.note: The /24 entry (Interface 3) is preferred over the /22 entry (Interface 2) because /24 is more specific — it defines a smaller, more precise address range containing the destination.

solution.result: The packet to 200.150.68.118 matches the /24 entry for 200.150.68.0/24 most specifically, so it is forwarded to Interface ID 3. Answer: A. 3.

questions.formula_note: Longest Prefix Match (LPM): the router selects the forwarding entry with the most specific (longest) subnet mask that matches the destination IP, found by ANDing the destination with each mask and comparing to the network address.
